text: Yeah, it's been a great day.
What about you?
Nice.

But, you know, that's what happens.
Yeah, it really is.
Well, I'll start by giving a little introduction of myself and a little bit about the team.
Then we can dive right in and get to know you a little bit more.
Just a little note.
Unfortunately, I had a call added at the half hour.
So I only have a half hour.

So I'm the managing director at GrowthX.
I run the sprint team, which is what this interview is for.
You may have already gotten a little bit of a download on this, but I'm just going to go over it to make sure.
The structure is when we get a client that comes in through sales.
The client then pays for the sprint.
Which is an eight-week period where we really build the AI pipeline or basically a sort of an agentic system to be able to create content.
And then we build a content strategy for them and we really show them.
It's like, you know, the proof is in the pudding kind of a thing where they get to see this is what it would be like to work with us.
These are the kinds of things that they would get out of it.

And we try as quickly as possible to start to get results, both from a GEO and SEO perspective to be able to then show, hey, we know what we're doing, we're representing your brand well, and we're starting to drive traffic to your site.
And at that point, then they have the option to either stop there if they're not interested, or they move over to phase two or the growth phase, which is where they now sign a year-long contract.
So that means that the sprint cycle is a couple of different things.
First and foremost, though, it is a part of the sales cycle.
And that is an important...
Element here is that it's still sort of a continuation of the initial sales conversation that they have.
And so it really is a combination of account management, showcasing what GrowthX is about, showcasing content strategy, SEO strategy, and really being that partner for them.
Because a lot of these clients are either they don't have any content specialist, or you're talking directly to some of the founders.
They're a small startup, and they're just starting to build this out.
And so in many cases, they are looking to us for not just the content creation side, but also the strategy side.
So you're sort of showing that.
And then last but not least, of course, is actually showing the process and the results.
So that's kind of the, in a nutshell, what the sprint is.
And what you specifically would be doing is you would be an engagement manager, which is...
A fancy way of saying account manager, but with a very strong strategic element to it.
So you're interfacing with the client.
You'd be the main point of contact there, but you would also be interfacing internally with managing editors.
Each client has a specific managing editor.
They are really helping build the pipeline, but you are building the strategy itself.
And then in between all of that, in some cases, we bring engineering in to tweak or change or build specific elements of the pipeline.
Any questions so far?
Got it.
Well, thank you for the explanation.
I do.
I have one question, I guess, to start with.
How many client touch points do we have within these eight weeks?
So it's one a week, so eight.
Eight.
Okay.
Really interesting.
So...
You said about working with the team, managing the team, and building that pipeline.
So when we talk about pipeline, we're talking about content pieces?
Yeah, when we're talking about pipeline, we're talking about a system, or we like to call it like an engine, that is the output is content pieces, yes.
But what the pipeline actually is, is a series of agentic workflows that do specific things.
So one is like doing research, for instance, know, next would be doing drafting the outline, and the next would be doing this, and that would be a fact checker.
And each one of those is a piece of what the full pipeline is.
But the pipeline is sort of the word we use for the whole thing.
Okay, okay.
Yeah, it would be really interesting to learn more about that.

You know, there's PSEO and all kinds of other things.
We do not do that in the sprint.

We just focus purely on, we're going to do some blog posts in an editorial style.
And we, around week four, we get to three articles.
And then by week five, we're doing five articles a week.
And then we finished the whole thing with five, five articles a week.
So by the end of their stay, they've published probably around 20 pieces.
20 pieces.

What specific skills or specific capabilities that we're looking for.
So the first one I'm going to ask about is if you are juggling, let's say, five to ten clients a week.
Um, yeah, I guess, um, that would be pretty much how I go about things.
Okay.
Do you use any kind of, uh, project management tools?
Uh, do you, when you're saying I prioritize my work, what does that look like?
Are you creating an Eisenhower box?

Okay.

Talk to me a little bit about a project that you have owned, uh, end-to-end, from beginning to end, that you had to report to leadership about.
Okay.
So that's, that's the context.
Tell me about one of these projects where things were not going, maybe as you were hoping, and you had to manage the situation.
So walk me through an experience like that.
Yeah, sure.
Um, I had a client, um, they engaged.
We a lot of authority for informational content, but there was an update at some point and they lost all of that.
So when we turn things around, we actually turn things around for the service pages.
It was bringing leads.
So it was a really good story.
At the end, the client was really happy.
The leadership was really happy, but there was a period from three to six months, there was just a lot of anxiety about like, are we going to do this?
And I think sometimes it's all about getting a little bit of a sanity check, being open and vulnerable with the team about, I think this is how we should do things, but it's bold.
So what does everyone think about it?
Yeah.
So that was a big one for me.
And it was a million page website.
Wow.
So tell me a little bit about you.
You're saying you said risky decisions and you just said bold.
I'm curious to understand.
Give me a little flavor there.
Like what were some risky things?
What were some bold decisions you guys made?
We de-indexed hundreds of thousands of pages.
Oh, de-indexed hundreds of thousands of pages.
Wow.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Sorry, I didn't go more in the weeds here.
I ask questions that are very in the weeds, but I'm trying to keep it high level.
That's okay.

So basically what we did was a content consolidation project where we did a big analysis, performance analysis, and then we kind of broke it down to see what was most interesting about each page, what was most valuable, and what should we keep and what should we eliminate.
And that's what we did.
So we broke it down by directory.
Like I said, it was...

It a healthcare site, so we had different verticals within, and yeah, we finalized after a while, it was great, and all of that was while implementing new strategies for new verticals they were launching, such as weight loss and so on.
So that was one of the most challenging projects that I ever worked on.
Okay.
Well, good on you for completing that.
That's incredible.
Okay.
I mentioned the fact that you, in this role, you have to balance a lot of different things in terms of internal and external stakeholders.
So tell me a little bit about a project where you did have to do something like that, you had multiple specialists or departments that you were interfacing with.
How did you make sure that everybody had visibility and that everything was really organized and on track?
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's an interesting question.
I hope my answer is not too basic, but that's kind of what I learned.
Post and share channels, not DM or direct emails, and then follow up a lot if you're not getting the answers that you need right away.
So that's the way to get people accountable.
But also, it's easy to just DM someone, forward something, and things get lost that way, I find.
So I'm all about spending the time that needs to be spent composing the right messages to people, where all the information is there, and it makes it easy to follow up.
That's pretty much how I like to work.
Okay.
And in this context, you're also using something like ClickUp to track tickets, anything like that?
Yeah.
So that's what I'm using right now.
But again, it could be Notion.
could be, you know, any other tool.
Yeah, I got you.
Okay, cool.
All right.
Well, my last sort of broader questions, and I'm going to jump back to some of these other ones to get a little bit more detail, but I just want to make sure I get to this one as well.

growthx is, in my opinion, really the tip of the spear when it comes to what you can do and what people are starting to get to when it comes to using AI intelligently, not just because there's a million and one solutions out there that create a bunch of AI content, and it's not very high quality.
And also, nobody reviews it.
Growthx perspective on this is, number one, the AI can be better if you take the time to build a really good system.
And then number two, nothing ever goes out without having a person review it, right?
So that's kind of like a little bit of our ethos.
I'm sharing this because my question is about, broadly speaking, your usage of AI and automation and your current knowledge of AI and automation.
There is no wrong answer to this.
I just want to know what your baseline is around what have you discovered or built recently?
What is your usage like on a day or weekly basis?
Talk to me a little bit about that.

I took one of those prompt engineering courses.
I use for absolutely everything.
The most basic things to creating schema code and things like that.
I really enjoy, but I'm not.
I'm to a bunch of different tools.

I have.

I just, I like ChatGPT.
And I mean, I talked to friends.
I tried a different one.

I just really got used to ChatGPT.

mean.

What would you communicate with the client?
Like if you're like, hey, I'm trying to get this done, but I'm a little delayed.
These are some challenges that I'm having.
How would you communicate that?
That's tough.
So one of the things that I, I mean, I try to set myself up for success, right?
I'm not going give the client a deadline that I know it's going to be hard to reach.
So I might give the client one deadline and my internal team a different one.
Most likely that's what I'll do.
Okay.
Great.
Yeah.
I mean, that tends to work.
Yeah, sure.

Great.
Super.

think that's, you know, improving, but yeah, I, all of that, of the long answer to tell you, I think I'm very confident and comfortable.

I still cannot identify what's the prompt, that kind of stuff that is frustrating.

Still a lot.

I got you.

Your profile for me is very interesting because you do have kind of the perfect mix of the two things that we're looking for, which is somebody who has account management experience and then somebody who has actual real deep SEO expertise.
I want to know from your own words, what makes you a good account manager?
I always put myself in other people's shoes, no matter who they are, really, as a professional, as a friend, as a human.
So in terms of account management, I build strong relationships, not just with my clients, but with my colleagues.
Z were asking about, you know, how...
How can you get things done when people are being unresponsive?
I always make good friends at work.
I know it's harder these days in a remote environment and with a ton of priorities, but I enjoy taking time, getting to know people.
I'm a people person and I'm accountable.
Nothing makes me happier than sharing good results.
I'm energized by that.
And that comes across in my work.

I think if you were to ask other people about me, how I work, this is coming from other people, not from me, but people apparently can get excited about SEO and performance based on the things that I report and because of my level of excitement when the experiments work.
So I think it's that.
I've been working in tech my entire career.
Oh, yeah.
Before starting SEO, I had customer service, customer success roles.
I was a translator, so I've been around for a while, and I have a very diverse experience.
So I think that translates quite well.
Right now, my role was not meant to be client-facing, which was the first time that I was just doing project work.
And that changed shortly after I started.
I'm brought on sales calls and QBRs and everything that typically the account managers do.
I'm not an account manager at the moment, but yeah, people count on me.
Yeah, they see that you have that ability.
Yeah.
Okay, great.
All right, well, we have about four minutes left, so I'm going to pass the mic back to you.
Do you have any last questions for me?
Yeah, so...
I was looking at your LinkedIn.
It seems like you've joined the team recently as well.
I would love for you to share, like, what are you excited about and what challenges are you finding?
Good question.
I'll start with the challenges.
The challenges are that, especially in this space, doing the sprint, it's very fast.

You're, you know, unlike probably most of the experiences that you've had in the past where you get a long period to build a relationship, and if it's a little shaky, you have a lot of time to rebuild that trust.
You don't really have that here.
You have to, you know, within two weeks, in week two, you're building a content strategy.
By week three, you're giving them a piece of content for them to test.
By week four, you're already ramping up content production.
By week six, you're showing them results.
And by week eight, you're saying, all right, let's go.
You know, so that ramp, even though it's two months, it's, it's.
Incredibly compacted.
And you're doing that with depends, you know, but it could be six to 10 clients at once.
And that can be challenging.
So that's why the first question I asked is like, how do you structure your work week?
How do you organize yourself is an important one, because you need to be very organized.
You need to be able to do all the things that you're talking about.
Like those soft skills are super important.
The excitement of the results, the confidence in your abilities, all very, very important.
But organizing yourself is a very core element of this.
And it can feel overwhelming because of that.
So I think that that is a bit of a challenge, is it can feel overwhelming because it goes very fast.

The second part that can sometimes make you feel a little overwhelmed is that this is, of all the companies I've worked for, which I've now worked for a lot, this is definitely the most technologically advanced.
And I'm not talking about their product.
I'm talking

Talking about internally, like how people are using technology, the people around me are using Claude and all these different LLMs and all these different tools and creating MCPs that connect their Slack to this other thing.
Like it's unreal.
And it's incredible because what you said about ChatDBT gives you all that productivity.
That's the goal very clearly.
It's productivity.
It's tracking.
It's making sure that we have everything noted down.
And that's actually kind of a good and a bad.
Like it can feel overwhelming and you're learning.
It's like drinking from the fire hose.
But it's a lot.
So, yeah, I've been here a little over two months and I'm still catching up on a lot of things.
The good sides.
This company is, you know, like probably a lot of other startups, but not everyone is like this, is truly a meritocracy.
Meaning like if they see you're good, they will give you more.
Opportunities.
When I started, I was not in this role that I'm in.
then within about a month, they were like, hey, you seem really capable.
Why don't you lead this team?
I was like, okay, this is huge, but all right, let's do it.
And I think that's very much the mentality.
And I personally really like that.
I think they're going to see what you're capable of, and then they're going to give you more responsibility, more capability.
They're going to support you, not throw you in there and then figure it out.
But yeah, I think that's a big piece of it.
I think another element is that it is truly a very, very good product.
They have an eight-week wait list of clients.
Everybody wants to work with GrowthX.
It is a good product.

And I think that they have, you know, as much as I've worked now for probably six or seven startups, it's definitely one of the more that I feel confident of, like, this could go pretty far, which is...
Exciting.
There's a lot, I'm not going to, I can't share what's going to happen.
There's a lot about to happen with the product that's going to change things a lot again, all in good ways.
But they really have a mentality of move fast, try things quickly, and grow and build from it, but never lose the focus on quality.

And I think that is a great, great element of this, because there's a lot, as I said at the beginning, there's a lot of companies out there creating very half-assed products and expecting the AI to do everything.
And I think there's a good balance of those two.
know, like, yes, use technology, but don't be a dummy.
You know, put humans where they need to be.
So, yeah, I'm afraid I do have to jump, but it was great to chat with you, Vivian.
I really appreciate it.
And we'll be in touch.
Sounds great.
It was great to meet you.
Bye-bye.
to meet you.
